resolver_decisions.evidence: Methods section explicitly states 'Cross-sectional data from the May 2020 COVID-19 survey (COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Release) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study were analyzed' with n=5412 adolescents. Paper analyzes U.S. ABCD Study data.
resolver_decisions.rationale: While the paper is formatted as a Letter in JAMA Pediatrics, it is a primary analysis of ABCD Study data meeting the core FT-IC1 requirement: 'Paper analyzes data from the U.S. ABCD Study.' The letter format is a publication type distinction, not an exclusion criterion. Per 2L-criteria.md §2.1, sub-samples and analyses of ABCD data qualify. C2 is correct.

resolver_decisions.evidence: Cross-sectional analysis of ABCD May 2020 survey examining associations between screen use modalities (texting, social media, video chat, gaming, streaming, browsing) and mental health/resiliency factors (mental health, stress, social support, coping). Reports regression coefficients for associations (Table 2).
resolver_decisions.rationale: This is original empirical ABCD analysis meeting FT-IC5 criterion: 'Original empirical ABCD analysis: cross-sectional... [reporting] associations involving smartphone use or social media use.' The paper reports inferential statistics (linear regression with β coefficients) linking screen time variables to non-SM outcomes (mental health, stress, social support, coping behaviors). The letter format does not negate the empirical nature of the analysis. C2 is correct.

resolver_decisions.evidence: Methods: 'Self-reported screen time data were collected via the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey... After summing the time spent across each modality, the total screen use weighted average was calculated.' Results report only total screen time (mean 6.1±5.2 h/day) with no separate estimates for texting, social media, video chat, or other YSTS modalities reported anywhere in results, tables, figures, or supplement.
resolver_decisions.rationale: Per binding rule B2: 'Paper uses YSTS but reports only a summed total screen time estimate (or a single composite) with no per-modality breakdown anywhere → FT-IC2 = NOT_MET (FT-EC2).' Although the survey captures per-modality data and the coders could extract individual YSTS items, the paper analyzes and reports exclusively on composite total screen time. No β/OR is reported for any qualifying smartphone/SM item (texting, social media, video chat, etc.). C2 is correct—B2 applies.

resolver_decisions.evidence: Paper models bidirectional associations: (1) psychiatric symptoms predicting later screen time ('Clinical levels of problems predicted higher levels of weekday... and weekend... nonsocial screen time'); (2) screen time predicting later psychiatric symptoms ('Comparable magnitude associations were observed for social and nonsocial media use predicting future psychiatric symptoms, suggesting bidirectionality'). Both directions with reported coefficients.
resolver_decisions.rationale: Per binding rule B20: 'Bidirectional / cross-lagged model where SM is both predictor and outcome → FT-EC7 does NOT apply.' This paper explicitly examines both (a) psychiatric symptoms→screen time and (b) screen time→psychiatric symptoms in longitudinal models. Because SM appears as both predictor and outcome, FT-EC7 cannot apply regardless of whether predictors are purely demographic. The bidirectionality disqualifies FT-EC7 per B20. Both coders correctly identified bidirectionality; C2 correctly concluded FT-EC7 does not apply (false).
